output:
**Here is a high-level summarized insight of a research field Machine Learning.**

Modular, skill-library robots store a map of their own competences - which low-level skill works, when, and how well - and a high-level controller uses that map to trade strategic value against execution confidence. The DeepMind human-level table-tennis agent is the sharpest instance of this design, but its self-knowledge is fit to the AGGREGATE training-opponent ball distribution and frozen at deployment. We name the resulting failure mode "Opponent-Induced Competence Drift": when a previously-unseen human steers ball position, speed, and spin into the robot's weak spots, the per-skill competence estimates become miscalibrated exactly in the regime that decides the match, and the controller makes silent, systematically wrong skill selections because the robot does not know that it does not know. The open problem is to make self-knowledge LIVING - recalibrated online, opponent-conditioned, from only the few hundred irreversible balls of a single match - and to feed that recalibrated, uncertainty-aware competence into a risk-sensitive controller that can defer to a safe fallback when its self-knowledge is stale.

---

**[Question 1] - What is the problem?**

Can a competitive table-tennis robot convert its static, training-time skill descriptors into online, opponent-conditioned competence estimates - recalibrated from only the few hundred balls of a single unseen match - and feed them to a risk-sensitive high-level controller that selects skills better than the static-descriptor baseline?

---

**[Question 2] - Why is it interesting and important?**

The central conceptual contribution is a design principle for any skill-library embodied agent: treat competences not as fixed scalar descriptors but as BELIEFS - distributions that are updated from live evidence conditioned on the actual operational context. Today's modular policies (options, skill libraries, mixtures of motor primitives) measure each skill's reliability once, offline, over an aggregate distribution, and then trust that number forever. Opponent-Induced Competence Drift shows why that is brittle: the moment the deployment distribution is actively shaped by an adversary or a novel user, the stored reliability map is wrong precisely where it is consulted most. Reframing the competence map as a posterior that is recalibrated in situ turns self-knowledge from a static artifact into a control signal, and it does so without retraining the underlying skills.

The problem also stresses a regime that most adaptation research avoids: extreme few-shot adaptation under irreversibility. A real competitive match offers only about 200-400 balls, every error immediately scores a point, there are no resets, and the opponent is previously unseen. An agent that can extract a calibrated, opponent-conditioned self-model from that little evidence - and that knows when its self-model is still too uncertain to act on - is solving a much harder and more realistic problem than multi-episode meta-RL with abundant resets. The ability to know WHEN one's own competence estimates are stale, and to defer, hedge, or escalate accordingly, connects directly to safe RL and human-robot trust: an agent that quietly overtrusts a degraded skill is dangerous, while one that recognizes degradation can fall back to a conservative action.

Finally, the impact generalizes well beyond table tennis and is cheap to obtain. The same drift afflicts surgical robots (patient-specific reliability of each manipulation primitive), assistive robotics (per-user distribution shift), and aerial systems (turbulence-degraded maneuver reliability). Because the mechanism is a small add-on that sits on top of a FROZEN skill library and controller, it is a low-cost modular retrofit rather than a redesign, and it opens concrete future tracks: competence meta-learning, coupled opponent-and-self modeling, and multi-match calibration priors that accumulate across opponents in a continual/lifelong setting.

---

**[Question 3] - Why is it hard?**

The hardest obstacle is sample starvation under non-stationarity. With roughly 200-400 outcomes per match spread over K opponent types (say 20) and S skills (say 8-12), the worst case is only about 5-30 outcomes per (skill, opponent-type) cell. A flat-prior frequentist estimate cannot reliably separate a true 60 percent success skill from a 40 percent one at that sample size (standard error near 0.1-0.2), and adaptive conformal intervals are wide early in the match. The shrinkage strength toward the static prior (the concentration parameter eta) is therefore load-bearing AND opponent-dependent, and tuning it by in-match cross-validation is circular because the very evidence you would validate on is the scarce evidence you are trying to use - this is the primary statistical risk.

Three further difficulties compound it. (a) Credit assignment: a lost point confounds skill miscalibration with strategic choice, opponent quality, and motor noise, so attributing failure to the competence map specifically is non-trivial - sim counterfactual replay partially resolves this, but a real robot remains confounded. (b) Real-time and safety: the full recalibrate-then-select pipeline must run under about 5 ms per ball (achievable with closed-form Beta or O(1) conformal updates), and the deferral mechanism must not be exploitable - an opponent should not be able to farm cheap points by forcing the robot into safe fallbacks, so defer frequency must be capped. (c) Non-stationary target: a strong opponent adapts back, creating a two-agent adaptation game in which the recalibrator may chase a moving target and the adaptive-conformal coverage bound (loose at small horizon T) degrades.

Why naive approaches fail: raw success-over-attempts counting has standard deviation around 0.1-0.2 at 5-30 samples, so the static prior is actually MORE reliable until roughly 100+ samples accumulate; without shrinkage the estimate overfits noise. Ignoring the opponent embedding averages over opponent types and is therefore wrong on exactly the match-deciding skills the opponent is targeting. And a risk-neutral controller acting on a fresh high-variance posterior over-commits - a skill estimated at 0.65 plus or minus 0.30 wrongly beats one at 0.60 plus or minus 0.05. Why it is nonetheless tractable: the skill library and foundation policies stay frozen (no policy retraining), the addition is only a few-thousand-parameter module, a controlled simulator can MANUFACTURE and directly MEASURE the shift (ground-truth per-skill success probability is available), the underlying machinery is mature (adaptive conformal inference, Beta-Bernoulli posteriors, CVaR bandits), and the design has built-in collapse and placebo controls that make every claim falsifiable.

---

**[Question 4] - Why hasn't it been solved before?**

This problem sits in the gap between four literature neighborhoods, each of which owns a piece but none of which occupies the target cell.

1. Robot table tennis. The DeepMind 2024 source agent is the first human-level competitive system; Abeyruwan et al. 2023 (i-Sim2Real) addresses sim-to-real of stroke EXECUTION, not skill-selection adaptation; Mulling, Kober, Kroemer, and Peters 2013 use a mixture of motor primitives with OFFLINE selection over a fixed ball distribution; Buchler/Guist et al. (MPI, around 2020) focus on low-level muscular motor control. None recalibrate the self-competence map online, opponent-conditioned, within a match.

2. Hierarchical RL and skill libraries. Sutton, Precup, and Singh 1999 (Options) use static initiation sets with no runtime calibrated success probability; Bacon, Harb, and Precup 2017 (Option-Critic) learn termination, not opponent-conditioned competence; Vezhnevets et al. 2017 (FeUdal) have a manager that issues goals but no competence models; Oudeyer, Kaplan, and Hafner 2007 use competence-progress for intrinsic motivation that is self-directed, not opponent-conditioned. Competence here is treated as static or only slowly self-improving.

3. Opponent modeling and few-shot within-match adaptation. He et al. 2016 (DRON) infer the opponent's TYPE, not the robot's own per-skill success; Foerster et al. 2018 (LOLA) need opponent gradients and multiple episodes; Finn et al. 2017 (MAML) and Duan/Wang et al. 2016 (RL^2) adapt the action policy end-to-end, require meta-training across opponents, are OOD-fragile against a "previously unseen human," and expose no explicit calibrated competence over a frozen library; Albrecht and Stone 2018 (survey) catalog opponent-behavior modeling while leaving the agent's own reliability map untouched.

4. Calibration/UQ and risk-sensitive control. Gibbs and Candes 2021 (Adaptive Conformal Inference) give passive marginal coverage under slow drift, not an embodied self-competence posterior under adversarial shift; Chow and Ghavamzadeh 2014 and Tamar et al. 2015 (CVaR) assume the risk estimates are already known and stationary; Bellemare, Dabney, and Munos 2017 (C51) and Dabney et al. 2018 (QR-DQN) model value distributions over stationary training data; Sunderhauf et al. 2018 provide offline UQ for robotics.

THE UNCLAIMED CELL: online, opponent-conditioned recalibration of the robot's OWN per-skill competence estimates, from only the few hundred rally balls of a single irreversible match against a previously-unseen adversarial opponent, fed to a risk-sensitive skill-selection controller that DEFERS to a safe fallback when recalibrated confidence is low. Skill libraries assume static/slowly-improving descriptors; opponent modeling targets the opponent's policy rather than the self-competence map; calibration is passive/offline/i.i.d.; risk-sensitive control assumes the risk estimates are correct. This is a new mechanism class (live Bayesian/conformal self-competence recalibration under adversarial non-stationarity) applied to a new object (the agent's own skill-reliability map inside a frozen modular library) in a new regime (single-match, few-hundred-sample, irreversible).

DELTA versus DeepMind 2024: that system does collect skill descriptors offline and online and performs an execution-aware trade-off, but (i) the descriptors are fit to the AGGREGATE training-opponent ball distribution, (ii) they are NOT updated opponent-conditioned within a match, (iii) they carry no uncertainty that is propagated into selection, and (iv) there is no CVaR/deferral hedging against stale self-knowledge.

Three undercut rebuttals. (1) Adaptive conformal prediction (Gibbs and Candes 2021; Angelopoulos and Bates 2023) yields marginal coverage on a future prediction, not a success-probability signal an action-selecting controller can optimize against; moreover an opponent STEERS balls to weak spots, which is a directed, non-exchangeable shift, and the method has never been applied to embodied competence-driven selection. (2) Meta-RL (RL^2, MAML) adapts the action policy, not an explicit interpretable competence map over a FROZEN library; it requires meta-training across opponents, is OOD-fragile against a genuinely unseen human, and produces no calibrated uncertainty for CVaR. (3) Competence-aware/introspective robotics (Jiang et al. 2012, "knows what it knows"; Oudeyer 2007) define competence as a static offline property, with no within-match opponent-conditioned recalibration and no risk-sensitive deferring controller.

---

**[Question 5] - What are the key components of my approach and results?**

Design philosophy: non-destructive modularity. Freeze the skill library, the per-category foundation policies, and the base high-level controller. Add exactly two components - an Online Recalibrator (ORC) and a Risk-Sensitive Controller (RSC) - such that at zero adaptation budget the system degenerates EXACTLY to B1 STATIC (the authors' own no-adaptation-limit implementation).

Online Recalibrator (ORC). Per-ball feature x in R^33: ball position/velocity/spin (3D each), end-effector position (3D), joint configuration (7D), time-to-contact, and table-relative geometry; optional PCA to about 15D. The outcome label y for skill s is binary: a legal return landing in the target region AND no unforced error within the next 2 shots, plus a secondary coarse point-won/lost label that feeds strategic value. An opponent embedding z in R^8..16 is cold-started from an offline Gaussian prior over training-opponent embeddings and updated online. Two update options: Option A is an EMA over a fixed offline feature-encoder phi (interpretable distributional summary); Option B (recommended) is a recursive Bayesian categorical posterior over K=20-50 prototype opponent types (k-means centroids), with p(type=k | x_1:t) updated via per-type KDE likelihood and the embedding taken as the posterior mean (microsecond-scale). Per-skill success model, Variant 1 (recommended primary): Adaptive Conformal Prediction (Gibbs and Candes 2021; Barber et al. 2023) - an offline base scorer (small random forest or logistic regression) plus a per-match adaptive threshold alpha_t, giving a loose marginal coverage bound under bounded-drift shift at O(1) per ball; note that directed adversarial steering violates exchangeability and the finite-sample gap of order sqrt(log T / T) widens at small T, consistent with the degradation noted in Q3. Variant 2 (secondary/ablation): a Bayesian Beta-Bernoulli model per (skill s, opponent-type k) with theta^s_k ~ Beta(alpha, beta), cold-started from the static descriptor scaled by concentration eta, updated softly with weight w_k(z_t) = p(type=k | z_t), yielding closed-form mean and variance.

Risk-Sensitive Controller (RSC). Objective: s* = argmax_s [ V(s) - lambda * CVaR_alpha(loss | s, x, z) ], where V(s) is the offline strategic value (expected point-win probability given a legal return). Under the Beta model, CVaR has the closed form 1 - BetaQuantile(alpha, alpha^s, beta^s); under ACP it is read from the interval width. The controller DEFERS to a pre-designated safe fallback skill when no skill beats the fallback under the risk-adjusted objective OR when the posterior variance exceeds tau (for example 0.04). A Thompson-sampling variant is kept for ablation, and lambda is swept over {0, 0.1, 0.5, 1, 2}.

Data and environment. Primary platform is a DeepMind-style MuJoCo table-tennis simulator (i-Sim2Real / GoalsEye lineage) with a 7-DoF arm. The opponent library is extended factorially: placement (3) x spin-type (3) x speed (3) x predictability (2) = 54 profiles; 36 are in-distribution and used to fit the static descriptors, and 18 are HELD-OUT to manufacture a genuine shift. The simulator exposes GROUND-TRUTH per-skill success probability, enabling direct expected calibration error (ECE). In addition, 5-10 adversarially-trained human-proxy opponents exploit specific weaknesses (for example serving where topspin success drops to about 40 percent although the descriptor claims 70 percent), run against both static and adaptive variants. Optional real-robot validation uses human volunteers under IRB/ethics approval with written informed consent and appropriate physical safety oversight (barrier guards, emergency stop, trained operator present); it measures the adaptation curve and confirms a sub-5 ms decision time; the volunteer pool's age, skill level, and demographic composition are pre-registered and reported, and defer frequency is logged against opponent strategy to confirm the cap is not exploited.

Metrics. Match win-rate; points-per-game; adaptation curve (rolling win-rate over the first 50/100/200/300 balls); per-skill ECE (10-bin) and reliability diagrams at ball counts {20, 50, 100, 200, full}; skill-selection regret versus an oracle; return rate and unforced-error rate; CVaR of point loss (alpha=0.1); and defer frequency versus actual failures.

Baselines. B1 STATIC (the paper, the no-adaptation limit); B2 ORACLE (true per-skill success, upper bound); B3 MARGINAL-RECAL (recalibrate WITHOUT the opponent embedding); B4 RISK-NEUTRAL (lambda=0, no defer); B5 NAIVE-FREQ (raw success/attempts, no Bayes/conformal/shrinkage).

Key ablations. AB1 opponent-embedding on/off; AB2 ACP versus Beta; AB3 lambda sweep; AB4 defer on/off; AB5 ball-budget {25, 50, 100, 200, 300}; AB6 prior concentration eta {1, 3, 10, 30}; AB7 K {5, 20, 50}; AB8 opponent adapts mid-match.

Falsifiability controls. (1) B1 collapse: setting eta -> infinity, lambda=0, and defer-off must reproduce STATIC's selection sequences, ECE, and win-rate identically (within floating-point tolerance); this is a by-construction unit test behind an --adaptation-off flag and must pass first. (2) Placebo pair, BOTH legs required. Leg 1 (structure-destroying): shuffling outcome labels or randomizing z each rally must produce NO win-rate or calibration gain. Leg 2 (stationary regime): testing against an in-distribution opponent with no shift must leave recalibrated ECE approximately equal to static with gain near zero. Failure of either leg is publishable negative evidence. (3) Info-budget: the gain over STATIC must increase monotonically with the ball budget from 0 to 300 - zero at budget 0 (equals STATIC), approaching ORACLE at 300 - and must dominate B3 and B5 beyond roughly 50 balls (paired t-test/Wilcoxon across seeds); no advantage over B5 at 300 would falsify the value of principled modeling.

Expected outcomes. Against held-out and adversarial opponents we expect the adaptive system to recover a substantial fraction of the STATIC-to-ORACLE win-rate gap after about 100-200 balls, with per-skill ECE on targeted skills dropping sharply once the opponent embedding resolves, while the in-distribution and placebo conditions show no gain - jointly demonstrating that the improvement comes specifically from opponent-conditioned recalibration of self-competence rather than from extra capacity or tuning. Compute budget: 54 profiles x 5 seeds x 30 matches = 8,100 sim matches at about 10 minutes each, parallelized over about 20 CPUs (about 3 days wall-clock); the offline opponent-encoder costs about 1-2 GPU-days on an A100; recalibrator inference is CPU-only; total roughly 3-4 engineer-months, plus an optional 2-3 week real-robot study with 10-20 volunteers. A key validity check: the 18 held-out profiles must be genuinely OOD, verified by confirming that static-descriptor ECE is higher on the held-out set BEFORE any adaptation experiments are run.

---

## Background and Current State of the Art

Modern competitive robot table tennis (DeepMind 2024) is built as a hierarchical, modular policy: a library of low-level skill policies (forehand topspin, backhand targeting, forehand serve, and so on), each specialized from a shared per-category foundation policy, sits beneath a high-level controller that selects among them. Crucially, the system stores "skill descriptors" - offline and online summaries of each skill's strengths, weaknesses, and limitations - and the controller uses them to perform an execution-aware trade-off, occasionally preferring a strategically weaker action when it is more confident of executing it. This is a major advance over earlier work that treated execution and strategy separately: Mulling et al. 2013 selected among motor primitives offline on a fixed ball distribution, while Buchler/Guist et al. and Abeyruwan et al. 2023 concentrated on the low-level motor and sim-to-real execution problem.

In parallel, the hierarchical RL literature formalized reusable skills (Options; Option-Critic; FeUdal) and intrinsic competence signals (Oudeyer et al. 2007), but these treat a skill's applicability as a static initiation set or a slowly improving self-property, not as a quantity that must be re-estimated against a specific adversary in real time. Opponent modeling (DRON, LOLA, the Albrecht and Stone survey) and meta-RL (MAML, RL^2) study within- and across-episode adaptation, yet they adapt the action policy or model the opponent's behavior, and they do not maintain an explicit, calibrated, opponent-conditioned map of the agent's OWN reliability. Finally, calibration and risk-sensitive control (adaptive conformal inference; CVaR optimization; distributional RL; offline UQ for robotics) supply the statistical machinery for uncertainty and risk, but they assume either passive i.i.d.-like prediction or already-correct, stationary risk estimates. The current state of the art therefore has all the ingredients - skill libraries, execution-aware selection, opponent models, calibration, risk-sensitive control - but no system that fuses them into live, opponent-conditioned recalibration of self-competence under single-match adversarial shift.

## Key Findings and Analysis

The framing analysis yields three load-bearing findings. First, the failure is structural, not incidental: because skill descriptors are fit to the aggregate training-opponent distribution and frozen at deployment, an opponent who steers ball features into the robot's weak regions induces miscalibration that is largest precisely where the controller relies on it most - Opponent-Induced Competence Drift. The controller's strategy-versus-execution trade-off is only as good as the competence numbers it consumes, so stale numbers produce silent, systematically wrong selections.

Second, the problem is well-posed and falsifiable. It has a clean no-adaptation limit (it must reduce exactly to the published static controller), a manufacturable and directly measurable shift (a simulator with ground-truth per-skill success and held-out opponent profiles), and a built-in placebo pair (structure-destroying randomization, and a stationary in-distribution regime) that together isolate the claimed mechanism from capacity and tuning artifacts. This combination is what separates a genuine mechanism from a story.

Third, the difficulty is concentrated in one place - sample starvation under directed non-stationarity - and the proposed machinery is matched to it: principled shrinkage from the static prior (Beta-Bernoulli with concentration eta) or distribution-free adaptive coverage (ACI), opponent-type conditioning to avoid averaging over the very heterogeneity that matters, and risk-sensitive selection with deferral to avoid over-committing on fresh high-variance posteriors. Naive frequency counting and risk-neutral selection are predicted to fail in characterized ways, which makes the comparison informative regardless of outcome.

## Novel Insights and Recommendations

The core insight is to treat a robot's competences as BELIEFS rather than fixed descriptors - distributions updated from live, context-conditioned evidence and consumed by a controller that hedges against its own uncertainty. We recommend: (1) build the two-module, non-destructive add-on (ORC + RSC) on a frozen skill library so the contribution is cleanly attributable and cheaply deployable; (2) make ACI the primary success model for its distribution-free guarantees under adversarial shift, with Beta-Bernoulli as an interpretable secondary; (3) condition on a recursive opponent-type posterior rather than a single global recalibration, since the drift is opponent-specific; (4) couple recalibration with CVaR-based deferral so that recognized staleness triggers a safe fallback rather than an overconfident error, while capping defer frequency to prevent exploitation; and (5) gate all experiments behind the collapse test and the two-leg placebo, and verify held-out OOD-ness (higher pre-adaptation static ECE on held-out profiles) before running the main study. Promising follow-ups include multi-match calibration priors that accumulate across opponents (continual/lifelong competence learning), coupled opponent-and-self modeling, and transfer of the principle to surgical, assistive, and aerial robots where per-context primitive reliability drifts. Extensions to such high-stakes domains require domain-specific safety analysis and independent ethics review; the present proposal is scoped to sports robotics.

## References and Evidence

1. Google DeepMind, "Achieving Human Level Competitive Robot Table Tennis," 2024 (source paper).
2. Abeyruwan et al., "i-Sim2Real," 2023 - sim-to-real of stroke execution.
3. Mulling, Kober, Kroemer, Peters, "Learning to Select and Generalize Striking Movements in Robot Table Tennis," IJRR 2013 - mixture of motor primitives, offline selection.
4. Buchler, Guist et al. (MPI), muscular/pneumatic table-tennis robot, around 2020 - low-level motor control.
5. Sutton, Precup, Singh, "Between MDPs and semi-MDPs: Options," Artificial Intelligence 1999.
6. Bacon, Harb, Precup, "The Option-Critic Architecture," AAAI 2017.
7. Vezhnevets et al., "FeUdal Networks for Hierarchical Reinforcement Learning," ICML 2017.
8. Oudeyer, Kaplan, Hafner, "Intrinsic Motivation Systems for Autonomous Mental Development," IEEE TEC 2007.
9. He et al., "Opponent Modeling in Deep Reinforcement Learning" (DRON), ICML 2016.
10. Foerster et al., "Learning with Opponent-Learning Awareness" (LOLA), AAMAS 2018.
11. Finn, Abbeel, Levine, "Model-Agnostic Meta-Learning" (MAML), ICML 2017.
12. Duan, Wang et al., "RL^2: Fast Reinforcement Learning via Slow Reinforcement Learning," 2016.
13. Albrecht, Stone, "Autonomous Agents Modelling Other Agents: A Survey," Artificial Intelligence 2018.
14. Gibbs, Candes, "Adaptive Conformal Inference Under Distribution Shift," JASA/NeurIPS 2021.
15. Angelopoulos, Bates, "Conformal Prediction: A Gentle Introduction," 2023.
16. Barber et al., conformal prediction beyond exchangeability, 2023.
17. Chow, Ghavamzadeh, "Algorithms for CVaR Optimization in MDPs," NeurIPS 2014.
18. Tamar et al., "Optimizing the CVaR via Sampling," AAAI 2015 (and 2012 work).
19. Bellemare, Dabney, Munos, "A Distributional Perspective on Reinforcement Learning" (C51), ICML 2017.
20. Dabney et al., "Distributional RL with Quantile Regression" (QR-DQN), AAAI 2018.
21. Sunderhauf et al., "The Limits and Potentials of Deep Learning for Robotics," IJRR 2018.
22. Jiang et al., "Knows What It Knows" line of competence-aware learning, 2012.
23. Kumar et al., "Conservative Q-Learning for Offline RL" (CQL), NeurIPS 2020.
24. Galichet et al., "Exploration vs Exploitation vs Safety: Risk-Aware Multi-Armed Bandits," ACML 2013.
25. Tamkin et al., distributional/risk-aware bandit and exploration work, 2019.
